confidence.disclosure: Ibn Battuta is the most-travelled medieval human we know of, and his Rihla — dictated to the Andalusian scribe Ibn Juzayy in Fez in 1354–55 — is the longest first-person travel record from the pre-modern world. Year-precision is good for the famous milestones: leaving Tangier in June 1325, reaching Cairo in spring 1326, his three pilgrimages, the seven years at the Delhi court (roughly 1334–41), the years in the Maldives (1343–44), the Chinese journey (1345–46), the return to Tangier in November 1349, the West African journey of 1352–53, and the dictation of the Rihla in 1355. But scholars (especially Ross Dunn and H.A.R. Gibb) have shown that some sections — particularly the Anatolian itinerary, parts of the Palestine description, and the Chinese chapters — may be confused, conflated, or partly borrowed from earlier travellers like Ibn Jubayr. Some researchers doubt he reached Beijing or even all the East African ports he claims. The grade is B, not A, because of these reliability issues. The locations are kept where the Rihla places them; uncertainty is flagged where appropriate.

sources: Ibn Battuta, Rihla (Tuhfat al-Nuzzar fi Ghara'ib al-Amsar wa Aja'ib al-Asfar), c. 1355
H.A.R. Gibb, The Travels of Ibn Battuta, A.D. 1325-1354 (Hakluyt Society, 4 vols, 1958-94)
Ross E. Dunn, The Adventures of Ibn Battuta (1986; revised 2012)
Tim Mackintosh-Smith, Travels with a Tangerine; The Hall of a Thousand Columns; Landfalls (trilogy 2001-2010)
David Waines, The Odyssey of Ibn Battuta (2010)

journey.event: Born on 24 February 1304 (17 Rajab 703 AH) in Tangier, on the northern coast of Morocco, into a family of Maliki qadis. He receives the standard education of his class in jurisprudence and the religious sciences.

On 14 June 1325 (2 Rajab 725 AH), at the age of twenty-one, leaves Tangier alone on the great pilgrimage to Mecca. He says farewell to his parents — neither of whom he will see again — 'with sorrow on both sides, like a bird that leaves its nest'.
Travels east along the North African coast, joining a pilgrim caravan in Tlemcen. Falls ill with fever and ties himself to his saddle to keep moving — the first of many close brushes with death.
Reaches Tunis. Marries his first wife, the daughter of a Tunisian official, somewhere on this leg of the journey — the first of many marriages he will make and dissolve along the way.

Arrives at Alexandria in Egypt in spring 1326. Marvels at the great lighthouse, of which a corner had recently fallen into the sea. Meets a holy man, Burhan al-Din the Lame, who tells him: 'I see that you love to travel through foreign lands. You will visit my brother Farid al-Din in India, my brother Rukn al-Din in Sindh, and my brother Burhan al-Din in China. Greet them from me.' Ibn Battuta takes this as a prophecy and decides to fulfil it.
Reaches Cairo, then the largest city of the Islamic world. Spends about a month and writes one of the most vivid descriptions of the medieval metropolis we have. Calls it 'the mother of cities'.

Heads south up the Nile and across the desert toward the Red Sea port of Aydhab, intending to cross to Jeddah. But the local ruler is in revolt against the Mamluks and the route is blocked. Forced to turn back to Cairo.

Travels overland from Cairo through Gaza, Hebron and Jerusalem and arrives in Damascus on 9 August 1326. Spends Ramadan there. Studies hadith with several local scholars and obtains a teaching certificate that has actually survived in Cairo's Al-Azhar archives — modern verification of the Rihla's chronology.

Joins the great Damascus pilgrim caravan and reaches Mecca in late 1326 to perform his first hajj. He has now completed the original purpose of his journey. He is twenty-two and has been on the road eighteen months. He decides not to go home.
Travels into Iraq, joining a caravan across the Arabian desert. Visits Najaf and the shrine of Ali. Then continues through Basra, Khuzistan, Isfahan, Shiraz and Tabriz — a long swing through Iran.

Reaches Baghdad and meets the last of the Mongol Ilkhans of Iran, Abu Sa'id, then on a royal progress through his realm. (Modern scholars note that some of Battuta's descriptions of the Tigris cities are copied from Ibn Jubayr's Rihla of 150 years earlier.)
Returns to Mecca and stays in the holy city for two or three years (1327/8–1330), leading the quiet life of a pious devotee — but his temperament cannot bear it for long.
Embarks at Jeddah and sails down the Red Sea, then follows the Yemeni coast to Aden, the great trading hub linking Arabia to East Africa and India.
Sails south down the East African coast (the Swahili coast). Visits Zeila, Mogadishu — where he describes the sultan and the famous local cuisine — and continues to Mombasa.

Reaches Kilwa in modern Tanzania, the southernmost point of his African journey. Calls it 'one of the most beautiful and well-constructed cities in the world'. The ruins of Kilwa are now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Sails back north up the African coast, then across the Arabian Sea to Oman, and up the Persian Gulf to the great trading port of Hormuz. Returns overland through southern Persia to Mecca for a third pilgrimage.

Performs his third pilgrimage. Hearing of the wealth and generosity of Sultan Muhammad ibn Tughluq of Delhi, decides to seek employment at the Indian court — but takes a wildly indirect route to get there.

Sails from Syria to the Anatolian coast and travels across the Turkish lands of the petty post-Seljuk emirates. Visits dozens of towns including Antalya, Eğirdir, Konya, Sivas, Erzurum and Bursa. He is welcomed everywhere by the Akhi brotherhoods of Turkish craftsmen, who specialise in lodging strangers. (Scholars suspect his Anatolian itinerary as recorded is geographically jumbled.)
Reaches the Black Sea port of Sinope and crosses to the Crimea on a Genoese ship.
Crosses the Eurasian steppe with a caravan and reaches Sarai, capital of the Golden Horde, the Mongol khanate of the Russian steppe. Meets the khan Öz Beg.

Joins the retinue of one of Öz Beg's wives — Bayalun, a daughter of the Byzantine emperor Andronikos III — who has been given permission to return to her father's house in Constantinople to give birth. He thus becomes one of very few medieval Muslims to visit the Christian capital, where he meets the emperor and tours Hagia Sophia from the outside.

Returns from Constantinople through the steppe and continues east. Visits Bukhara, Samarkand and Balkh, all still showing the scars of the Mongol invasion of a century earlier. At Samarkand he meets the Chagatai khan Tarmashirin.

After crossing Afghanistan and the Hindu Kush in winter — losing several companions to the cold — Ibn Battuta and his party reach the Indus River on 12 September 1333 by his own dating. Modern scholars (Dunn) doubt this exact date and suggest he actually arrived two years later in 1335; the distances are too great for the time elapsed.

Reaches Delhi and is appointed qadi (Maliki judge) by Sultan Muhammad ibn Tughluq, on the strength of his Mecca training and his fluent Arabic. Begins seven years at the Delhi court — paid lavishly, but always at risk from the sultan's notoriously unstable favour, which can turn from gifts of gold to summary execution overnight.

Years of high office and high anxiety in Delhi. He marries multiple Indian wives, fathers children, falls in and out of favour, is briefly suspected of conspiracy and placed under guard. He sees the sultan execute scholars, Sufis and his own relatives. He says of Muhammad ibn Tughluq: 'Of all men, he is the fondest of giving gifts and of shedding blood.'

The sultan, suddenly restored to favour and trust, appoints Ibn Battuta as his ambassador to the emperor of China. He is to lead an embassy of fifteen Chinese envoys back to their country with rich gifts. (The Rihla dates this to 1342, but Dunn argues it was 1341 to allow time for the subsequent Maldives years.)

Travelling to the coast with the embassy, the caravan is ambushed by Hindu rebels. Ibn Battuta is separated from his companions and robbed of everything but his trousers. After harrowing weeks he rejoins them at the fortress of Daulatabad and they make their way to the port of Cambay in Gujarat, then south to Calicut on the Malabar coast.

At Calicut, the embassy boards Chinese junks for the voyage east. On the eve of departure a storm wrecks the ships in the harbour. The gifts for the Chinese emperor are lost. Ibn Battuta, ashamed to face the sultan empty-handed, decides to flee Indian waters and never return to Delhi.

Sails to the Maldive Islands, where he stays for nine months. The local Muslim queen and her ministers — short of trained jurists — appoint him chief qadi. He marries into the ruling family (four wives at once, at the upper limit of Islamic law) and tries to enforce orthodox dress codes on the Maldivian women, which fails dismally.

Leaves the Maldives after a quarrel with the ruling vizier and sails to Sri Lanka. Climbs Adam's Peak, the mountain held sacred by Hindus, Buddhists and Muslims alike, and visits the ruby mines of the interior.

Resumes his eastward journey. Sails along the Bay of Bengal to Bengal and Burma, then around the Malay peninsula to the sultanate of Samudera Pasai on the northern tip of Sumatra, the easternmost outpost of Islam at that time.

Sails on to China, landing at the great southern port of Quanzhou (which he calls Zaitun). Describes Chinese paper money — a wonder unknown in the Islamic world — and the immense river junks. Claims to travel inland as far as Beijing, though most modern scholars doubt this and believe the Beijing chapters are partly literary borrowing.
Begins the long return journey westward — back through Sumatra, Calicut, the Persian Gulf, and the Arabian peninsula. Performs another pilgrimage at Mecca.

Reaches Damascus in 1348, where he had not been for over twenty years. Learns that his father has been dead fifteen years. The Black Death is sweeping through Syria and the Middle East; he reports death tolls of two thousand a day in Damascus, eleven hundred a day in Cairo. Stops in Homs, Gaza, Aleppo and Egypt, finding cities depopulated by plague.

After a final detour to Sardinia, returns at last to Tangier in November 1349, nearly a quarter of a century after leaving home. Discovers his mother has died of plague only a few months before his arrival. Does not stay long.

Crosses to al-Andalus to fight as a volunteer on the frontier of Muslim Granada against the Christian kingdoms. Visits Ronda, Marbella, Malaga and Granada itself, then under the Nasrid sultan Yusuf I, completing a sweep through the last Muslim land in Europe.
Returns to Morocco and prepares for his last great journey: across the Sahara to the empire of Mali. Sets out from Sijilmasa in southern Morocco with a salt caravan crossing the desert.
Reaches Walata in modern Mauritania after a brutal desert crossing of two months. Continues south through the empire of Mali at its imperial peak under Mansa Sulayman.

Spends eight months in the imperial capital of Niani, on the upper Niger. Describes the court ceremony of Mansa Sulayman, the gold trade, and the relative status and freedom of West African Muslim women — which, by his Maghrebi standards, scandalises him.
Visits Timbuktu and Gao on the Niger bend, then turns north across the Sahara again, this time with a slave caravan, before reaching Morocco at the end of 1353.

Returns to the court of the Marinid sultan Abu Inan Faris at Fez. Sultan Abu Inan orders him to dictate the story of his thirty years of travel to the young Andalusian scholar Ibn Juzayy, who shapes the material into the literary work known as the Rihla.

The Rihla is completed in December 1355 (Dhu al-Hijja 756 AH), three months after Ibn Juzayy began writing it down. Ibn Juzayy himself dies very shortly afterwards, in his thirties. The book attracts little attention in its own time.
Spends his last years serving as a qadi in some unidentified Moroccan town. Almost nothing is known of these final years; the Rihla was his life.

Dies in Morocco in 1368 or 1369, aged about sixty-four. Tradition holds that he was buried in his birthplace, Tangier, where a tomb is shown today. His Rihla was almost forgotten until a manuscript reached Europe in the early 19th century, since when he has been recognised as the most travelled human of the pre-modern world — by his own count some 75,000 miles, across forty-four modern countries.
